structured.title: Confusing Percentage Debate Continues
structured.overview: The conversation is a fragmented, tense exchange about how to calculate percentages and reductions. One participant argues that a $600 charge reduced to $10 represents a 600% reduction, while another pushes back with repeated اعتراضs and profanity. The dialogue is unclear in places, but the central topic is a disagreement over math and what counts as “enough” in the discussion.

transcript_segments.text: A piece? Down the middle. No.
transcript_segments.speaker: SPEAKER_1

transcript_segments.text: No. No. No. No. That enough. The fuck you mean that's not enough, man? I got a lifestyle to maintain here.
transcript_segments.speaker: SPEAKER_4

transcript_segments.text: Do you know what it At the prem doesn't a tribe?
transcript_segments.speaker: SPEAKER_1

transcript_segments.text: That's a different way of calculating it. There's two ways of calculating percentage. If you have a $600 charge and you reduce it to 10, that's a 600% reduction. That you know I own
transcript_segments.speaker: SPEAKER_5

apps_results.content:
## Confusion over the split
- “Exactly” asks about splitting a piece
- Response says “down the middle” is wrong
- More back-and-forth rejects that idea

## Not enough to cover lifestyle
- One person says “that enough”
- Reply complains: “I got a lifestyle to maintain”
- Discussion turns tense and frustrated

## Percentage calculation debate
- One side questions a calculation method
- Mentions two ways to calculate percentage
- Example: $600 charge reduced to 10

## Final point on reduction
- Calls it a 600% reduction
- Implies ownership or control: “I know I own”
